text: BARBON, a chapelry in the p. of Kirkby-Lonsdale, in Westmoreland, 3 m. NNE of Kirkby-Lonsdale. Area 4,690 acres. Pop. 315.
BARBONA, a village of Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, in the deleg. and 20 m. SSW of Padua, on the l. bank of the Adige. Pop. 2,500.
BARBONNE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Marne, cant. and 6 m. S of Sezanne. Pop. 1,276.
BARBOUR, a county in the state of Alabama, U. S., between the Chattahoochee river on the E, and the Pea river on the W. Pop. in 1840, 12,024. The cap. is Clayton.
BARBOURSVILLE, a town in Knox co., Kentucky, U. S., 119 m. SE by S of Frankfort, on the N side of Cumberland river. Pop. 224.—There are several villages of this name in the United States.

BARBU'DA, one of the Caribbee group in the W Indies. It is a low level island, of an oval form, 15 m. in length, by about 8 m. in breadth, lying 30 m. NE of Antigua, and 60 m. NNE of St. Christopher's; between N lat. 17° 54' and 17° 29'; and W long. 61° 56' and 61° 43'. In the Colonial Magazine [vol. v., p. 225], considerably greater dimensions are assigned to it, viz., length 20 m.; breadth 12 m.; circumf. 70 m. The W coast is remarkably low and level. From the centre of the island to the N end, the land rises gradually to the height of 170 ft. above sea-level; beyond which it suddenly dips into a level plain, extending 2 m. from N to S, and 6 m. from E to W. The E side is so high as to be seen at 5 leagues' distance. Nearly the whole surface is covered with a thick luxuriant vegetation, "so high as to preclude the possibility of seeing 20 yards to the r. hand or l. of the road" by which one passes across the island. A narrow neck of land extends along the whole W coast, forming a lagune about 7 m. in length, and 1 m. in breadth, which is entered by a narrow channel at the N end. On the E side of this lagune, 5½ m. from its entrance, stand the castle and village, in N lat. 17° 45′ 24". Nearly the whole coast is foul and dangerous, being beset at almost every point with shoals and reefs under water, particularly on its N, NW, and SE points. Indeed no vessel of any size can approach, even in the day time, nearer than 7 or 8 m., without running imminent risk of touching the reefs. This island is held under a long lease from the Crown, by the Codrington family. The pop. amounts to about 400 Negroes. No sugar is grown on the island, but about 500 acres are under Indian corn, cotton, and tobacco. The island is chiefly used as a grazing district for horses, mules, cattle, and sheep; and is well-stocked with deer. Turtle and fish abound on the coasts.—Mr. Goodlett in Naut. Mag., vol. iv., 1835.—Coleridge's Six Months in the W. Indies.

BARBUE, a river of Michigan, U. S., which enters Lake Michigan, in N lat. 43° 2', after a course of 60 m. from E to W.-Also a river of Upper Canada falling into Lake Erie. BARBURRA, BARBURA, or BARBUR, a port or anchorage of E Africa, in the Somauli territory, between Cape Guardafui and the straits of Bab-el- Mandeb, 130 m. E of Tajurrah. It is annually frequented from September to March, or during the SW monsoon, by about 100 vessels from different parts of India, during which time a fair is held on the sea-beach, and a temporary town rises, consisting of from 4,000 to 5,000 huts of an oblong form, about 6 ft. broad, by 9 ft. in length, and 5 ft. in height, and consisting of a roof of mats or half-dried skins, stretched over a few sticks. The principal import is cloth, for which the natives barter goats, coffee, gum, and ghee, or pay in dollars. Pilgrims and merchants from the interior here embark for Jidda and Mecca.

BARBY, a parish and v. in Northamptonshire, 6 m. NW of Daventry. Area of p., 3,700 acres. Pop. 640.—Also a town and district of Prussia, in Saxony, on the Elbe, near the influx of the Saale, 14 m. NW of Dessau. Pop. 3,300.

BARCA, a district of Northern Africa, bounded on the N by the Mediterranean; on the E by the Egyptian frontier; on the S by the Sahara or Desert; and on the W by the kingdom of Tripoli. It extends in length about 690 geog. m., or from 18½° to 31° E long.; and in breadth about 180 m., or from 28° to 31° N lat. The etymology of the name is uncertain. It has been explained as signifying 'a blessing,' and derived from the Arabic barac 'to bless.' Others have traced it to Barca, a brother of Queen Dido, who is supposed to have founded the city of Barca. The modern Arabs understand it as denoting 'the Place of hurricanes.' In ancient history it formed a part of that immense tract of country which bore the general name of Libya; and was then divided into two provinces, called Libya Cyrenaica and Libya Marmarica. The modern state and history of B. are very imperfectly known. It is described as a tract of dry barren sand, almost entirely destitute of vegetation, incapable of culture, and rarely yielding a spring of fresh water. A few spots of verdure, consisting chiefly of the different kinds of kali or glasswort, occasionally relieve the eye of the traveller, and furnish a slender refreshment to his suffering camel; and at long dreary intervals are found some fertile places, or oases, where the towns and villages are situated, and where a little millet, maize, and sometimes abundance of dates, are produced. The western parts are the most fertile. In some of the more desert parts, the surface of the ground is covered with a saline crust; and, in the regions towards the south, great quantities of petrified wood of various forms and sizes, even large trunks of trees, particularly of oak, are found in the sand.—Along the whole coast, and in many parts of the interior, are found numerous remains of classic architecture, - the splendid but melancholy monuments of a civilization and prosperity which have completely passed away. Among the towns on the coast are Bengazi, Cyrene, Teuchera, Barca, and Bonandria,—the five cities which conferred on this district in ancient times the name of Libya Pentapolis. Bengazi occupies the site of the ancient Berenice. It is a filthy and utterly decayed place, though built on the coast, close to the sea, and at the extremity BAR of a beautiful and fertile plain. Its present pop. does not exceed 2,000 souls. Cyrene is built on the edge of a range of hills rising about 800 ft. above a fine sweep of high table-land. We must refer the reader to Captain Beechey's interesting account of Cyrenaica, for a description of the antiquities of this city and the surrounding region. Teuchera or Tauchira, has likewise borne the name of Arsinoe and Cleopatris. It has no port, though close on the sea. Tolometa, the ancient Ptolemais, the port of Barca, still preserves its ancient walls. Demah is a very pleasant town, but is occasionally ravaged by the plague. The inhabitants of these maritime places, in their general character and customs, resemble the other natives of Barbary. They profess the religion of Mahomet; are considered as under the protection of the Porte; and are tributary to the pasha of Tripoli, or of Egypt, according as they approximate to either of these kingdoms; but little is known respecting their political state, or commercial intercourse.—The Barcan desert is separated from the Libyan, on the S, by a chain of rocky mountains among which the most considerable towns and villages are situated, and where the climate and soil are more favourable than in any other part of the country. The chief of these towns, which have been noticed by travellers, are Mogara, Ummesogeir, Siwah,-where very extensive ruins have been observed, and where the oracular temple of Jupiter Ammon is supposed to have been situated, -Mojabra, Melidilla, and Augila, which is mentioned by Herodotus as being ten days' journey from the city of the Ammonians, and which both in ancient and modern times, has been celebrated for the abundance and superior flavour of its dates. The houses in these towns are generally placed on the side of the mountains, and have much the appearance of caves in the rocks. The soil in their neighbourhood yields pomegranates, figs, olives, apricots, plantains, a little wheat, and a considerable quantity of rice of a reddish hue, but principally dates. A few sheep, goats, asses, oxen, and camels, are kept by the natives; but, for want of pasture, the cattle are frequently supported by the fruit of the date-tree. The inhabitants are engaged chiefly in agriculture and gardening; and sometimes carry on a petty traffic with the Arab caravans from the cities on the coast, or with those which pass between Fezzan and Egypt. Some, by these means, acquire considerable wealth; but they are in general remarkably poor and dirty; and are frequently almost entirely naked, their dress, at most, consisting only of a large wrapper of coarse woollen cloth. Sometimes, under this, they wear a white cotton shirt with wide sleeves, reaching to the feet, a Tunisian cap of red worsted or cotton, characteristic of the Mussulman, and slippers of the same colour. They subsist chiefly upon dates, rice, milk, flat cakes of unleavened bread, or thin sheets of paste fried in the oil of the palm-tree. They drink great quantities of a liquor made from the date-tree, which has an inebriating quality. Of the more central parts of Barca, scarcely any thing whatever is known. Few travellers have attempted to explore its pathless wastes of barren and burning sand, where they could have no other guide than the compass of the stars, and would be continually exposed to the cruel rapacity of the most savage and brutal of all the Arab race, aided by thieves, outlaws, and malecontents, from Tripoli and Egypt. The few wandering tribes who traverse these dismal regions, are described as peculiarly hideous in their aspect, ferocious in their manners, and meagre and ravenous in their whole appearance. They are wretched and indigent in the extreme; and subsist principally by plundering the date-villages, and levying contributions from the caravans which pass along the coast of the Mediterranean, or upon the borders of Libya. They are almost continually engaged in these predatory excursions; and are said occasionally to commit the most atrocious acts of cruelty upon those who fall into their hands.—Heeren's Historical Reflections. -Brown's Travels in Africa.-Hornemann's Travels in Africa.—Blaquiere's Letters.—Captain Beechey's Cyrenaica.
BARCA. See PTOLEMETA.
BARCA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Entre- Douro-e-Minho, and comarca of Viana, on the l. bank of the Lima, 15 m. N of Braga. Pop. 1,800.
BARCA-TROFA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Entre-Douro-e-Minho, on the r. bank of the Ave, 10 m. E of Villa-do-Conde, and 15 m. NE of Oporto.
BARCALIS. See BANCALIS.
BARCAMUDI, a village of Albania, on the S bank of the lake and 4 m. SE of the town of Janina.
BARCAROTA, or BARCARROTA, a town of Spain, in Estremadura, in the prov. and 25 m. S of Badajos. It has a castle; and in the vicinity is a mineral spring. Pop. 4,000.
BARCATTY, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Travancore, 40 m. ESE of Cochin.

BARCELLO'NA, or New Barcellona, a district of Venezuela, in the dep. of Maturin, on the coast of the Caribbean sea, consisting chiefly of extensive plains, covered with rich pasturage, and supporting large herds of cattle, the commencement of those llanos which, uniting with those of Caraccas, stretch southwards to the low alluvial plains of the Orinoco. It takes its name from its cap., a town situated on the l. bank of the Neveri river, in N lat. 10° 13′ 15″, W long. 64° 26′ 30″, about 2 m. from the sea, and 30 m. from Cumana. It was a place of considerable commerce about the beginning of the present cent., exporting immense numbers of cattle and horses, and large quantities of hides and jerked beef, besides salt, indigo, annetto, cotton, and cocoa. In 1807, its pop. was estimated at 15,000, of whom one-half were whites. It was also a great emporium for contraband goods. It still carries on an active trade with the Columbian archipelago, and particularly with the island of Trinidad. The number of vessels which entered the port of B. in 1840-1 was 21 belonging to the republic, of a total tonnage of 998 tons; and 8 foreign vessels, of a total tonnage of 350 tons.—Also a com. and village of Sicily, in the district of Castroreale. Pop. 2,430.

BARCELLOS, a town of Brazilian Guayana, or Para, on the Rio Negro, and the cap. of the captaincy of the Rio Negro till 1816. It is now in a ruinous state, and its pop. does not exceed 500.—Also a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Entre-Douro-e-Minho, on the Cavado, 13 m. W of Braga. Pop. 3,900.—Also a v. of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, on the Marahu.

BARCELONA, one of the four provinces of Catalonia in Spain; bounded on the NE by the prov. of Gerona; on the SE by the Mediterranean; on the SW by the prov. of Tarragona; and on the NW by the prov. of Lerida. It is about 100 m. in length from N to S; and 65 m. in breadth; and comprises 220 sq. leagues of surface. The northern parts of this prov. are intersected by ridges which detach themselves from the Pyrenees. The greater number of its streams flow from NE to SW, and fall into the Mediterranean. The most considerable of these are the Llobregat, the Bezos, the Tordera, the Tornarron, and the Segre. The singular form of the mountains, and brilliant vegetation of the valleys, give to this district an agreeable and picturesque aspect. In the mountains the climate is somewhat rude, their summits during the greater part of the year being con- BAR cealed in snows. In the plains and valleys the temperature is mild, and on the sea-shore the air is pure and healthy; but the climate generally is variable. The agricultural productions are corn, fruits, oranges, olives, and good wine. The cork-tree occurs in the forests; and the meadows afford excellent pasturage for cattle. Numerous flocks of sheep are reared, the wool of which is excellent. Game is plentiful; and bears and wolves abound in the wilder parts of the mountains. There is a considerable amount of manufacturing industry in this prov. Woollen cloths, gloves, hats, stockings, chocolate, soap, gunpowder, glass, musical instruments, and silk ribbands and handkerchiefs, are among the principal items of manufactured produce. Bleachfields and tanworks are numerous. The pop. was estimated by Mellado in 1844 at 442,848.—This prov. is divided into 14 partidos: viz., Arens, Berga, Granollers, Igualada, Manresa, Mataro, San Felin de Llobregat, Tanasa, Vich, and Villafranca del Panades, besides 4 comprised in the cap. It contains 544 pueblos; and 98,294 houses.

BARCELO'NA, the Barcinona of the Romans, and the cap. of the prov. of Barcelona, is one of the principal cities of Spain. It is situated on the Mediterranean, between the rivers Bezos and Llobregat, in a beautiful and fruitful country which forms an oblong irregular plain, encircled with hills on one side, and bounded by the sea on the other; in N lat. 41° 22' 43". The city is defended on one side by the shallowness of the sea, and on the other by numerous bastions, the approaches to which are guarded by advanced works. Its chief defence, however, is the citadel, a regular pentagon on the system of Vauban, which was erected in 1715 at the NE point, to overawe the inhabitants, and the fort of Monjuich on the top of a mountain at the SE point. The ramparts, -called the sea-wall and the land-wall, -embrace about threefourths of the town, and form a superb terrace from which there is a delightful view of the city and the surrounding country. At the end of the land-wall is the esplanade, a large open piece of ground turfed and planted with trees, and extending from the new gate to the citadel. Though some of the streets are sufficiently spacious, yet, in general, they are narrow and crooked. The squares are numerous, but small and irregular. The largest of them, the Pla de Palacio, is very spacious and elegant, decorated on one side by the governor's palace, on the opposite side by the exchange, on the other by the sea-gate and the custom-house, and on the N by a row of good houses. The architecture of the houses is, in general, pleasing and simple; they are for the most part built of brick, about four or five stories high, having large windows with balconies. Many of the houses have their fronts adorned with paintings in fresco; and several of the public buildings are remarkable, both on account of their external beauty, and the curiosities which they contain.—The cathedral, about 160 ft. long, and 62 ft. broad, has a magnificent appearance, but is yet unfinished. Twelve Gothic pillars separate the nave and aisles; and a large octagonal dome of Gothic architecture, with eight galleries, stands in the middle of the space between the choir and the great door. The sanctuary,—which stands over a subterranean chapel containing the relics of St. Eulalia,—is formed by ten pillars arranged in a semicircle, and enclosing the great altar, which is in the Gothic style, and of exquisite workmanship.—The convent of La Merci has a large church, with a Doric portal, and a fine front composed of two stories of Corinthian and Ionic architecture. Its cloister, which is 60 ft. square, is most superbly executed; and the portico, of sixteen arcades, which surrounds it, is supported by twenty Doric columns of marble. Above the portico is a spacious gallery, which has, on the outside, thirtytwo arcades, on Ionic columns of marble, ornamented with a balustrade of grey marble. The convent of St. Francisco has a large and handsome Gothic church, and a cloister adorned with paintings. The parish-church of Santa Maria del Mar, or St. Mary of the Sea, built in the 15th cent., is the finest in Barcelona. The principal altar is a rich assemblage of white, black, and mixed marble. The Hotel-deville contains a variety of excellent pieces of sculpture. The Palacio de la Diputacion, now the Audiencia, where the States of Catalonia assembled, is one of the handsomest edifices in the city, and contains the archives and charters of the crown of Aragon. The palace of the counts of Barcelona and the kings of Aragon, distinguished by the noble simplicity of its architecture, is now occupied by a nunnery and an academy of medicine. The governor's palace, built in 1444 as a market for cloths, was converted into an arsenal in 1514; and in 1652, Philip IV. made it the residence of the viceroys of Catalonia. The exchange is a rectangular building, 230 ft. long and 77 ft. wide; and has a noble and majestic appearance. The custom-house, the theatre, and the school-of-surgery, are the only other buildings deserving of notice. Besides these public buildings, there are six hospitals, a Casa de caridad or a charity-house, and an asylum. The poor employed in the casa de caridad average 1,500. The university of Barcelona was suppressed by Philip V; and the building is now used as barracks. The principal public library has 16,000 vols.; that of the convent of Santa Catarina, 20,000. The remains of those superb buildings with which the Romans decorated B., have almost wholly perished. The festivals and ceremonies of the church are particularly brilliant at B. Those which take place during "the holy week" are the most remarkable. The harbour of B. is formed by a kind of bay situated between the citadel of Monjuich and the city. At the beginning of the 16th cent. it was merely an open coast, with considerable depth of water: at present it is only a great basin, formed by piers, and kept up by solid quays. The depth of water is from 18 ft. to 20 ft.; but notwithstanding the exertions which are made to keep it clear, the basin is gradually filling up with sand: ships of any considerable size cannot enter, and frigates cannot come within half a league of it. The entrance is difficult and occasionally dangerous in consequence of a high bar formed at the place where the waters of the Bezos and the Llobregat mingle in the sea. The vessels now belonging to this port are chiefly employed in trade with Cuba and Porto Rico. In 1831, the number of vessels from foreign ports which entered was <math>128 = 15,135</math> tons; and of these 2,010 tons were under the British flag in 18 vessels. In <math>1849</math>, <math>1.896</math> vessels <math>= 140,369</math> tons, entered the port. Of these <math>1,732 = 114,314</math> tons, were Spanish; <math>33 =</math> 5,007 tons, were English; and <math>1 = 132</math> tons, was Austrian. The imports, under Spanish colours, consisted of corn, cotton, meal, and coals; from England, iron, machinery, and coals; the Austrian vessel was laden with timber. The situation of B. long rendered it one of the most flourishing and commercial towns in Spain. About 1,000 Spanish ships, of which nearly 120 belonged to B., annually cleared out of its harbour, and carried to America, and to the different maritime kingdoms of Europe, the productions and the manufactures of Catalonia. The articles of this export trade were silver, gold, and plain stuffs, silk stockings, cloths, printed calicoes, striped and flowered cottons, cottons of all kinds, plain and stained papers, firearms, laces, shoes, wines, and brandies. The imports were silks from Lyons and Nismes, silk stockings from Nismes and Ganges, cloths from Elbeuf and Sedan, jewellery from Paris, iron-ware from Forez, millinery from France, and cotton goods and dried cod from England. The export and import trade was said to amount annually to £1,750,000 sterling. The cotton goods manufactured in B. produced annually £442,510 sterling. Of these, one-twelfth was consumed in B.; two-twelfths in the other provinces of Spain; and two-thirds were shipped for the Spanish colonies. About 700,000 pair of shoes, at 2s. 1d. the pair, were annually exported from B. to Spain, India, and the Spanish colonies in America. Between the 13th and 16th cent., cloths made of woollen, linen, silk, cotton, and hemp, were manufactured in B. After a long interval of inactivity, its manufactures revived about the middle of the 18th cent., and were flourishing beyond all former example at the commencement of the present cent. The principal articles were printed calicoes, silk, silk stockings, ribbons, and silk galloons. The articles manufactured from silk were taffetas, twilled and common silks, satins, and velvets; besides these articles, laces, blonds, network, and tapes, were mafactured to such an extent as to occupy about 12,000 persons; gold and silver fringes and embroideries were likewise made. Cotton-spinning was introduced in 1790, and employed about 4,000 looms, and 10,700 persons. No less than 120,700 pieces of cotton stuffs of various kinds were annually manufactured, to the value of £242,510.—The chief exports from B. at present consist of about 12,000 pipes of wine, and 3,000 of brandy, to Cuba; 16,000 pipes of wine, and 6,000 of brandy, to South America; and about 30,000 bags of nuts, of an average value of 30s. per bag, to England. The aggregate value of the imports during three years ending in 1831 was only £420,000. —The bank of B. has a nominal capital of 1,000,000 dollars, of which 371 per cent. is paid up. The climate of B. does not seem to be so mild as it was at one period. The air is constantly charged with humidity, and E winds are very prevalent. Formerly snow never fell at B., but at present it snows every year; and the spring is always the worst season of the year. The intense heat of summer is moderated by the E wind; but the changes of temperature are both great and sudden. Autumn is the most delightful season.—The pop. of B. has fluctuated greatly. In 1806 it was estimated at 160,000; in 1819, at 81,445. A Manuel para los Viageros printed in March 1822, estimated it at 98,050. In Mellados Espana Geografica published in 1845, it is estimated at 112,046. History.] Barcelona was founded about 250 B. C., by the Carthaginians, who called it after their general Hamilcar, Barcas or Barcino. After having successively passed under the dominion of the Romans, the Goths, and the Moors, Barcelona was besieged, in A. D. 802, under its Moorish governor, Omar, by the generals of Louis, king of Aquitania. Having opposed a heroic resistance for seventeen months to the continual assaults of the besiegers, it yielded to the French arms only after its walls were demolished, and one-half of its inhabitants destroyed by famine or the sword. In 985 it was taken by the Moors, under Almansor, who burned the city, and carried into slavery almost all the inhabitants; but it was afterwards succoured by Count Borello. The rebellion of the Catalonians in 1465 against Don Juan, king of Aragon, was fostered in the cap. of the province. The king besieged it in vain in 1462; but on the 17th October 1472, after a siege of six months, it yielded to the superior force which he brought against it. The revolt of the Catalonians in 1640, exposed B. to new dangers. It maintained its independence for twelve years against the arms of the profligate Philip IV.; but was at last taken by Don Juan of Austria, in 1652, after a blockade and siege of ten months. In 1689 it opposed an ineffectual resistance to Charles II. In 1697, fifty days after the trenches were opened, it was taken by the French under Vendome, though the bravery of the inhabitants was seconded by a garrison of 12,000 men under the Prince of Darmstadt. Although the citi zens had sworn allegiance to Philip V., they invited the English and Dutch to deliver them from his yoke; and Charles, afterwards emperor, was proclaimed king in this city. In 1704, Philip, aided by the French, besieged B. in person, and took the fortress of Montjouy or Monjuich; but the English fleet compelled him to raise the siege on the 12th of May. In consequence of the treaty of Utrecht in 1713, Catalonia and the neighbouring provinces resumed their allegiance to Philip V.; but Barcelona refused to join in the universal submission, without first receiving a solemn guarantee that all the privileges of the principality would be preserved entire; and in 1714 sustained one of the most memorable sieges that history has to record. Feats of heroism worthy of the best ages of Rome, and efforts of individual courage which have perhaps never been surpassed, but by the modern inhabitants of Zaragosa, distinguished the dark and bloody night of the 12th Sept., in which the streets and houses of B. were filled with the mangled bodies of its warriors. In 1808 it was seized by the French general Duhesme. In 1827 it pronounced in favour of Don Carlos. Authorities.] Laborde's View of Spain, vol. i. - Bourgoing's Travels in Spain, chap. xxxiii.—Townsend's Travels, vol. ii.— Reichard, Guide des Voyageurs en Europe, vol. i.-Tour in Spain and Portugal in 1803, published in Philip's Voyages and Travels, vol. iii.—Espana Geografica por Mellado.
BARCELONA, a port on Lake Erie, in the state of New York, U. S., 51 m. SW of Buffalo. Pop. 300.

BARCELONETTA, the name of a new town or suburb adjacent to Barcelona. It is situated to the SE of the city, between the sea-gate and the lighthouse on the mole, and was built about the middle of the 18th cent. It is a complete square, with twenty-four regular streets, each about twenty-five feet broad: fifteen of these are direct and parallel, and are intersected at equal distances by nine streets. The houses, which are built of brick, are all one story high, and of the same height and width in front. The uniformity of the streets is a little varied by two squares. The inhabitants are chiefly soldiers, sailors, and people connected with the navy. Pop. 5,000.
BARCELONNE, a town of France, in the dep. of Gers, cant. of Riscle, on the Adour, 40 m. W of Auch. Pop. 1,190.

BARCELONNETTE, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Basses- Alpes.—The arrond comprises a superficial area of 112,659 hectares, and contains 4 cant., viz., Allos, Barcelonnette, Lauzet, and St. Paul. In 1831 its pop. amounted to 18,783; in 1836 to 18,709; and in 1846 to 18,561.—The cant. comprises 9 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 8,601.—The town or city of B., the cap. of the arrond., is situated on the r. bank of the Ubaye, in a rich and picturesque valley of the same name, 30 m. NE of Digne, at an alt. of 3,705 ft. above sea-level. It is the seat of a communal college, and an elementary, normal school. It has manufactories of silk, cloth, and caddis, and conducts an extensive commerce in grain, mules, cattle, and sheep. This town, which is supposed to occupy the site of an ancient Roman town, was rebuilt in 1230 by Raimond Beranger, and named by him in memory of his Barcelonian ancestry. It was captured by Amedee, Duke of Savoy, in 1388, and after frequent alternations was, with the valley, eventually ceded by the treaty of Utrecht, to the French, in 1713. Pop. in 1789, 2,158; in 1836, 2,154: in 1846, 2,207.

BARCELORE, or BARCUR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Canara, on a river about 4 m. above its entrance into the Indian ocean, 30 m. SW of Nuggur or Bedenore. It possesses a small port, and exports rice and pepper chiefly to Muscat, receiving in return horses and dates. It was formerly the cap. of a small independent state, governed by a female sovereign. It now belongs to the Dutch.
BARCENA-DEL-BARCO, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Alava, 30 m. WSW of Vittoria, on the r. bank of the Ebro.
BARCETESIOS, a mountain of Albania, in the sanjak of Valona, a ramification of the Pindus range, extending between the Desnitza and the Levkaritza.
BARCHESTON, a parish in Warwickshire, 1 m. BAR ESE of Shipston-on-Stour. Area 1,620 acres. Pop. with the hamlet of Willington, 193.
BARCHETA, a village of Spain, in the prov. and 5 m. NE of Valencia. In the vicinity are quarries of fine marble.

BARCHFELD, a town of Hesse-Cassel, in the prov. of Fulda, on the r. bank of the Werra, 8 m. NW of Schmalkalden. It contains a castle, which is the residence of the landgrave of Hesse-Philippsthal-Barchfeld. Pop. 1,400.

BARCILLONNETTE, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Alpes, arrond. of Gap. The cant. comprises 3 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 1,074. The village is situated on the Déoule, 11 m. SSW of Gap. Pop. 361.
BARCIS, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. of Friuli, on the r. bank of the Zelline, 18 m. ENE of Belluno.
BARCO (EL), or BARCO D'AVILA, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 35 m. WSW of Avila, on the r. bank of the Tormes. It is the cap. of a judicial partido, and has a thermal spring.

BARCOMB, a hundred and parish in Sussex.- The hund., which is in the rape of Lewes, comprises 3 parishes, and an area of 9,740 acres. Pop. in 1841, 2,475.—The p., which is 3 m. N by E of Lewes, has an area of 5,280 acres. Pop. 1,028.
BARCUS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrenees, cant. of Mauleon. Pop. 2,497.
BARCUT, or BARAKUT, a low coral island in the Red sea, 9 m. W 3 S from Barmosa Kebir island, in N lat. 19° 15'.
BARCZIN, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Posen, and circle of Szubin, on the r. bank of the Netze, 18 m. S of Bromberg. It has manufactories of cloth, and tanneries. Pop. in 1837, 586.

BARD, or Bardo, a small fortress in Piedmont, at the S entrance of the valley of Aosta, and 17 m. SSE of that town, on the l. bank of the Doria, between the towns of Verres and Donas. The valley is here suddenly contracted, and the river rushes through a gorge formed by vast rocks which almost close the entrance; the fort commanding this pass is consequently a strong military position, as was experienced by the French army in 1800, on their march to Marengo, when an Austrian garrison of 400 men held them in check for a considerable time.—Also a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Puyde-Dome, com. of Boudes. Pop. 210. There is a mineral spring here.

BARDAK-CHILI-KOI, or BARDAKLU, a village of Anatolia in the sanjak of Karahissar, between Eskishehr and Bulavadin, in a fine fertile valley. It contains about 100 houses, and there are remains of ancient buildings in the vicinity.

BARDEH, a village of Nubia, on the E bank of the Nile, to the S of Dakke. The mountains here approach both sides of the river, but those on the E bank are most elevated. They appear to be of volcanic formation, and rise in rugged chains or ridges.—St. John.

BARDEN, a township in the parish of Skipton, W. R. of Yorkshire, 8 m. NE of Skipton. Area, including Bolton-abbey chapelry, 10,110 acres. Pop. of B. 212.—Also a township in Haukswell p., N. R. of Yorkshire. Area 1,330 acres. Pop. 111.
BARDENBERG, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, 4 m. NNE of Aix-la-Chapelle, on the Wurm river. Pop. 1,500. There are coal mines in the neighbourhood.

BARDEWIK, or BARDOWICK, a town of Hanover, in Luneburg, on the Ilmenau, 4 m. N of Luneburg. Pop. 1,362. There are numerous market gardens in the vicinity, whence the city of Hamburgh is chiefly supplied with vegetables.
BARDFIELD (GREAT), a parish in Essex, 4 m. E of Thaxted. Area 3,400 acres. Pop. 1,120.
BARDFIELD (LITTLE), a parish adjoining the above. Area 1,970 acres. Pop. 375.
BARDFIELD-SALING, a parish in Essex, 5 m. NE of Great Dunmow. Area 1,510 acres. Pop. 381.
BARDI, a town of Parma, in the district of Borgotaro, 30 m. WSW of Parma. The princes of Doria have a magnificent castle here.

BARDIA, an island in the gulf of Siam, on the W coast of Lower Siam, opposite the mouth of the Tayung, in N lat. 10° 48'. It is about 15 m. in length from N to S, and is separated from the main by a narrow channel. There is a small town of the same name on the island.
BARDI-KADIJA, a fortress of Tunis, on the promontory of Vada, to the SE of the city of Tunis.
BARDINETTO, or BARDINEI, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. and 12 m. N of Albenga. Pop. 800.

BARDIS, a village of Upper Egypt, 3 m. SE of Girgeh, near the l. bank of the Nile.—Also a v. of Persia, on the road from the Russian frontier to Erzrum, and 12 hours from Kars. It stands near a stream of the same name which, running E, falls into the Arpachai.

BARDNEY, a parish in Lincolnshire, 4 m. SW of Wragby. Area 5,490 acres. Pop., with the hamlet of Southrow, 1,192. This place was anciently called Beardanum; and one of the oldest abbeys in the kingdom stood a little to the NW of the village of B., but no remains of it are now visible. There is a large burrow in the neighbourhood, which it is supposed marks the site of the burial-place of King Ethelred.
BARDO (AL), a small fortified town in the regency of Tunis, 13 m. NW of Tunis. Its inhabitants, about 4,000 in number, consist almost exclusively of families attached to the court.
BARDOLINO, a village of Lombardy, in the deleg. and 15 m. WNW of Verona, on the r. bank of the Lago di Garda. Pop. 2,000.
BARDONNECHIA', or BARDONNECHE, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. and 20 m. W of Susa, near the Doria river. Pop. 1,200.
BARDOS, a commune and v. of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrenees, cant. of Bidache. Pop. 2,468.
BARDOWAL, an extensive lake or lagune, in the isthmus of Suez, on the coast of the Mediterranean to the N of the Desert of Moving sands, 150 m. NE of Cairo.

BARDSEA, a township in the parish of Urswick, in Lancashire, 2½ m. S by E of Ulverstone. Pop. 263.—A canal was at one time projected from this place through Ulverstone, to Newry-bridge at the foot of Windermere lake.

BARDSEY, an island off the coast of Carnarvon, in N lat. 52° 45', W long. 4° 47', at the N point of Cardigan bay. It is about 2 m. in length, by 1 m. in breadth; and has a small harbour on the SE side, capable of admitting vessels of 40 tons burden. Pop. 90. There is a lighthouse on this island with a fixed and intermitting light at the height of 141 ft. above high water. The channel betwixt the island and the main is about 1 m. wide, and the tidal current in it is very rapid.—Also a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. SW of Wetherby. Area 3,380 acres. Pop., including that of the townships of Bardseycum-Rigton, part of Wike, and Wothersom, 469.
BARDT. See BARTH.
BARDWELL, a parish in Suffolk, 9 m. NE of Bury. Area 3,320 acres. Pop. 826.
BARE, a township in the p. of Lancaster, Lancashire. Area 180 acres. Pop. 120.
BAREDA, a village of Nubia, on the r. bank of the Nile, 75 m. S by W of Essuan. BAR

BAREEDY (CAPE), a moderately elevated promontory on the coast of the Red sea, in N lat. 24° 16', E long. 37° 38'. The face of the shore is here bounded by steep cliffs, and there are no soundings close to them.—Moresby.

BARE'GES, or BARRE'GES, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Pyrenees, and com. of Betponey, 12 m. SE of Argeles, 14 m. SSW of Bagnères, and 47 SE of Pau, on the Gave de Bastan, at an elevation of 4,180 ft. above sea-level. [Forbes.] It is celebrated for its mineral waters; and though merely a hamlet, which during the greater part of the year does not contain above 100 inhabitants, during the season of the baths, or from 1st June to 1st October, is visited by a considerable number of strangers. The hamlet is perched on the shelf of a mountain, in a great drift way through which the Bastan rushes precipitously; and so deep does the snow lie here in winter, and on so precarious a tenure do the people hold their dwellings amidst the storms and floods of this region, that the shopkeepers, migrating at the approach of winter, carry with them the wooden walls of their baraques or huts [Paris], or at least dismantle their houses, even to the window-sashes. The waters—which appear to have come into repute only so recently as the time of Louis XIV.—have three principal sources, which are distinguished as the hot, the temperate, and the tepid, and which supply 17 baths, 2 douches, and 2 large basins. The water of all these springs is clear, but of a nauseous odour and taste, and deposits an unctuous glairy substance. Their temp. ranges from 110° to 84°. [Taylor.]

BAREILLY, or BAREILI', an extensive district in the western part of the prov. of Delhi, in Hindostan; lying between the parallels of 28° and 30° N lat.; and bounded on the N by Moradabad; on the E by the hills of Kumaon; on the S by Shah-Jehanpur, and the reserved territories of the king of Oude; and on the W by the Ganges. Area 6,900 sq. m. Under the Mogul administration, this district, including Shah-Jehanpur, formed part of the circar of Budayun; and it formed a part of Rohilcund when conquered by the British, acting in name of the vizier of Oude, in 1774. In November 1801, it was ceded to the East India company by the vizier, then king of Oude. The great extent of the district, and the turbulent conduct of its Rohilla pop., rendered it expedient to subdivide it; and the W part, accordingly, now forms the collectorship of B.; while the E part forms that of Shah-Jehanpur. B. is in general a level, well-watered district. In 1815 it contained 3,362,022 begans fit for cultivation, but not cultivated; 4,458,380 begals in cultivation; and 3,558,899 begans of waste land; yet the soil and climate are fine; the basmati rice of this district is superior even to that of Patna, and its sugar and cotton are reckoned the best in India. Date-palms and plantains, walnuts, apple and pear-trees, and vines, are amongst the vegetable productions. In summer the heat is intense; but during winter, while the winds blow from the snowy ranges on the N, the therm. frequently falls below the freezing point. The inhabitants are, with few exceptions, Mahommedans. The Rohillah Afghans are a tall handsome race of men, and still betray a strong leaning to their original predatory habits. The principal native manufactures are salt, nitre, and cloth; and a considerable commerce is maintained with Kumaon and the countries to the N.—The principal towns are the cap. of the same name, Budayun or Badaun, Chundowsi or Chandausi, Cossipur, and Pillibit or Pilibhit.

BAREILLY, the cap. of the above district, is situated in N lat. 28° 23', E long. 79° 16'; 42 m. NW of the Ganges, and 700 m. NW of Calcutta. By travelling distance it is 129 m. from Agra; 345 m. from Benares; 805 m. from Calcutta; and 142 m. from Delhi. It chiefly consists of one street, nearly 2 m. in length. The British station is a quadrangular citadel to the S of the town. The number of brick and mud houses in 1822 was 12,263; of shops, 1,663. The pop. in that year amounted to 65,790, of whom 40,205 were Hindus; and 25,585 Mahommedans. A minutely detailed account of the various classes comprising this pop., given in the 1st vol. of the Royal Asiatic Society's Transactions, affords abundant proof of the superior industry of the Hindu to that of the Mahommedan. Of the Hindus, nearly four-fifths are engaged in retail trade and manufactures; of the Mahommedans, only about three-fifths. The principal manufacture is that of brazen water-pots. Cabinet-work is also beautifully made here; and the list of artisans includes carpet-weavers, brocadeweavers, seal-cutters, gold and silver-smiths, stirrup and harness makers, bow and arrow makers, coarse porcelain manufacturers, grain-roasters, metal-refiners, and leather-dressers. — Ayeen Akberry. — Mill's Hist. of British India.—Hamilton's E. I. Gazetteer.— Heber's India.—Parl. Reports.—Asiatic Researches.
BAREIRA, a town of W Africa, in the territory of Forricaria, on a river of the same name, in N lat. 9° 32'.
BAREITH. See BAIREUTH.
BAREMA. See BARIMA.

BAREMA-DAGH, a mountain range in Turkish Armenia, in the prov. of Diarbekir, under the 39th parallel. Its N flank forms a part of the S boundary of the basin of the Morad or Upper Euphrates. The Barema or Barimaer river descends from its S flank towards the Tigris.
BAREN, or BAARN, a village of Holland, in the prov. of Utrecht, 15 m. NW of Amersfoort. Pop. 1,150.
BARENBUCH, a village of Hessen-Homburg, in the lordship of Messenheim.
BARENBURG, a village of Switzerland, in the Grisons, 2 m. S by W of Andeer, near the r. bank of the Upper Rhine.
BARENSTEIN, a town of Saxony, on the Muglitz, 20 m. S of Dresden. Pop. 855. Tin is mined in the vicinity.—Also a v. of Saxony, in the district of Pirna. Pop. 449.
BARENTHAL. See BEHRENTHAL.
BARENTIN, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Pavilly, on the Astreberle. Pop. 1,788. There are paper manufactories, and cotton spinning works here.
BARENTON, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Manche, arrond. of Mortain. The cant. comprises 4 com. The com. and v. are 8 m. SE of Mortain. Pop. 3,106.

BARES (CAPE), or VARES, a promontory of Spain, on the N coast of Galicia, on the W side of the estuary of the Sor, 24 m. NW of Mondonedo, and 13 m. E of Point Estaca. The land between these two points is high and steep; and on the E side of the promontory is a deep bight known as the harbour of Bares or Barquero, which is sheltered from every wind except those from NE round to SE.
BARETE, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo Ultra 2da, 8 m. NW of Aquila. Pop. 1,000.

BARETUN (AL), or BARETON, a small port of Egypt, near the confines of Barca, in E long. 27° 12', now called Port Mhaddra, or Berek Marsa. In the neighbourhood are ruins supposed to be those of the ancient Parætonium.
BARETTI, a village in the Himalaya mountains, between the highest branch of the Jumna river, and the Bagirutti, an affluent of the Ganges.
BARETSWEIL, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 18 m. from Zurich, in the BAR bailiwick of Hinzel. Pop. 3,462, Protestants. Cotton and silk stuffs are manufactured here.

BARFLEUR, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Manche, arrond. of Valognes, cant. of Quettehou, 14 m. E of Cherbourg, on the Manche. Pop. 2,675. It was formerly a town of considerable importance, and the common port of embarkation or landing used by the kings of England when they crossed the Channel. In 1840, its harbour—which is dry at low water-was entered by 65 vessels of an aggregate burden of 1,179 tons; and its own shipping amounted to 98 vessels of 1,575 tons. Its chief commerce is in fish, both fresh and salted. The N jetty head light is in N lat. 49° 40′ 14", W long. 1° 15′ 26″.
BARFLEUR (POINT), or GATTEVILLE, the NE extremity of the peninsula of Colentin, in the dep. of Manche. The lighthouse on this headland is in N lat. 49° 41′ 52″, W long. 2° 15′ 48″.

BARFORD, a hundred in the NE of Bedfordshire, containing 8 parishes. Area 26,170 acres. Pop. in 1801, 5,137; in 1841, 6,330.—Also a parish in Norfolk, 41 m. N of Wymondham. Area 940 acres. Pop. 417.—Also a p. in Warwickshire, 3 m. S of Warwick. Area 1,490 acres. Pop. 849.
BARFORD (GREAT), a parish in Bedfordshire, 6 m. ENE of Bedford. Area 2,830 acres. Pop. 814.—Also a p. in Oxfordshire, 2 m. WNW of Deddington. Area 1,180 acres. Pop. 370.
BARFORD (LITTLE), a parish in Bedfordshire, 2 m. SW of St. Neot's. Area 1,190 acres. Pop. 190.
BARFORD (St. John), a chapelry in Oxfordshire, in the p. of E Adderbury, 21 m. W of Deddington. Area 480 acres. Pop. 126.
BARFORD (St. Martin), a parish in Wiltshire, 21 m. W of Wilton. Area 2,290 acres. Pop. 569.
BARFORD (St. MICHAEL), a parish in Oxfordshire, in the hund. of Wootton. Area 1,180 acres. Pop. 370.

BARFORD, a township of Lower Canada, in Stanstead co., between the townships of Barnstonfrom which it is separated by Coaticook river—and Hereford. It is well-watered by the numerous tributaries of the St. Francis, and abounds with excellent timber.
BARFORTH, a township in the p. of Forcett, N. R. of Yorkshire, 10 m. N of Richmond. Area 1,750 acres. Pop. 114.
BARFRESTON, or Barston, a parish in Kent, 6 m. S by E of Wingham. Area 360 acres. Pop. 125.

BARGA, a town of Tuscany, in the prov. and 35 m. N of Pisa, and 27 m. N of Lucca, near the l. bank of the Serchio. It is the cap. of the vicariate of B., and possesses a fine church. It has powder mills, and the environs afford fine jasper. Pop. 2,510.-The commune of Barga, comprising an area of 7,697 hectares, is enclosed in the duchies of Lucca and Modena, and contains a pop. of 6,790.

BARGE, a town of Piedmont, in the prov. and 10 m. WNW of Saluzzo, and 27 m. NW of Coni, on the l. bank of the Grandon. It has manufactories of fire-arms, and an active commerce. In the vicinity are quarries of slate. Pop. 4,000.

BARGEMONT, a commune and town of crance, in the dep. of Var, and cant. of Callas. The town is situated 7 m. NNE of Draguignan, on a wellsheltered hill, covered with vineyards and olive plantations, and has five annual fairs. Pop. 1,891.
BARGEN, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Berne, near the l. bank of the Saane, 3 m. SSW of Aarberg. Pop. 418, Protestants.

BARGHE, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 13 m. NE of Brescia, and 8 m. NW of Salo, in the Val Sabbia, on the I. bank of the Chiesa. It has a forge, manufactories of linen, and oilpresses. The environs abound with the mulberry. Pop. 560.
BARGO, a village of New South Wales, in Camden co., on a stream of the same name, which unites with the Nepean, in S lat. 34° 16'.
BARGOUCHAD, or PARGACHAD, an ancient town of Persia, the cap. of a small dist. of the same name, in the prov. of Erivan on the Aras.

BARGOUZIN, or BARGUZIN, a town of Siberia, cap. of the district of the same name, in the gov. and 250 m. NE of Irkutsk, on the river Bargouzin, which, after a course in a SSW direction of 240 m. falls into Lake Baikal, in N lat. 53° 15'. The town possesses a wooden fort. In the environs are thermal springs and salt lakes.
BARGU. See BURIAT.
BARGUE (Point), a promontory of Siam, forming the SW extremity of one of the islands in the delta of the Irawadi, in the district of Dalla, in N lat. 15° 45', and E long. 95° 32'.

BARHAM, a parish in Kent, 6 m. SE of Canterbury. Area 4,480 acres. Pop. 1,151.—Also a p. in Huntingdonshire, 53 m. NNE of Kimbolton. Area 700 acres. Pop. 107.—Also a p. in Suffolk, 4 m. NNE of Ipswich. Area 2,520 acres. Pop. 576.
BARHEAD. See BARRHEAD.
BARHOLME, a parish in Lincolnshire, 4 m. WNW of Market - Deeping. Area 1,230 acres. Pop. 165.
BARI, a town of Sardinia, in the division of Cagliari, near the r. bank of a stream which falls into the sea in N lat. 39° 55', and 45 m. NNE of Cagliari. It possesses a small port. Pop. 1,521.

BA'RI, the ancient Barium, a town of Naples, the cap. of the prov. of the same name, situated on a tongue of land which projects into the Adriatic, 50 m. NW of Tarentum, and 140 m. ENE of Naples, in N lat. 41° 8', E long. 16° 52'. Pop. 22,000. It is well-fortified, and defended by a citadel; and although generally ill-built, possesses a handsome cathedral, several parish-churches and convents, and an extensive arsenal. It has also a celebrated priory erected in 1087, and dedicated to St. Nicholas; and is the seat of a royal lyceum, a college founded in 1817 for the education of the young nobility, and a diocesan seminary. The manufacturing industry of the town -which is extremely active-comprises silk, cotton and linen fabrics, cloth, soap, glass, and a celebrated liquor named 'Aqua Stomatica-di-Santa-Scolastica.' The port affords good anchorage, and is well sheltered, but incapable from its shallowness of receiving large vessels; nevertheless B. carries on a brisk commerce with Trieste and the ports of Dalmatia, exporting oil, cotton, and grain, in large quantities. In 1834 the port possessed 638 vessels of a total burden of 14,288 tons, and navigated by 3,123 hands. B. is the see of an archbishop, metropolitan of the bishoprics of Bitonto-et-Ruvo and Conversano.-It is a town of great antiquity. Under the Roman republic, it enjoyed the privilege of being governed by magistrates of its own. It subsequently passed under the successive domination of the Saracens, Greeks, and Normans; the latter of whom made it the seat of monarchy. It has been three times almost totally destroyed; and as often rebuilt on the same site.

BARI (TERRA-DI), a province of Naples, in the Pouille; bounded on the N by the Adriatic; on the E and SE by the prov. of Otranto; on the SW by that of Basilicata; and on the W by the prov. of Capitanata, from which it is separated by the river Ofanto. Its length from NW to SE is 80 m.; and its average breadth 30 m.; comprising a superficial area of 1,711 sq. m. Its southern frontier is skirted by the SE branch of the Apennines; but, with the exception of some hills of inconsiderable elevation | silicata, in the dist. and 4 m. SSE of Melfi. It oc- BAR which occur in its easterly districts, it presents a generally level surface. It has no perennial streams of importance; but contains an ample underground supply of water at no great depth, and easily attainable for the purposes of irrigation; and numerous swamps occur in the tract of low land near the coast called the Marina di Bari, and remarkable for its luxuriance. The climate is dry; and in summer extremely warm, but is not generally insalubrious. The soil of this prov.-which is one of the richest, and with the exception of the Terra-di-Lavora the best cultivated in the kingdom-consists to a great extent of a calcareous substratum covered with a thick layer of vegetable mould of the greatest fertility, and producing grain, olives, oranges, citron, figs, pomegranates, nuts of all kinds, peaches, apricots, almonds, capers, the liquorice plant, carobe-tree, tobacco in small quantities, excellent cotton, lint, and hemp. It affords also several excellent species of wine, particularly the muscat of Trani, the zagarese of Bitonto, and the white wines of Terlizzi; and the oil of some of its localities is highly esteemed. Wood is generally scarce; but pasturage is rich and abundant, and the rearing of horses, asses, cattle, sheep-the wool of which is extremely fine, - goats, and pigs, forms one of the principal occupations of the rural inhabitants. The culture of bees and of the silk-worm is very limited. The sea-coast abounds with fish. The saltsprings of Barletta yield a large amount of salt, and the annual produce of the nitre beds is estimated at 12,000 cwts., and might easily be raised to 50,000 cwts. Ship-building is carried on in all the maritime towns; and there are some linen-manufactories at Molfetta; but excepting at Bari manufactures form an unimportant branch of the industry of the prov. The commerce, which especially in Bari and Barletta is extremely active, is chiefly maritime; and consists in articles of local produce. The harbours, from want of depth, are incapable of receiving large vessels. A good carriage road extends along the coast; and another, crossing the Ofanto at Canosa, connects Bari with the intermediate and more southerly towns. —The prov. is divided into 3 districts—named from their principal towns—Bari, Barletta, and Altamura, comprising 37 circondario, and 153 communes. The government is administered by an intendente or chief magistrate, who, although the courts of judicature are at Trani, resides at Bari, and 37 local or inferior officers. The pop., comprising a large proportion of Arnaouts or Abanians, who have established themselves on the coast, amounted in 1820 to 360,639; in 1837, to 448,943.
BARI. See PUNJAB.

BARIA, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Rio Ne gro, which joins the Cababuri with the Pacimoni, branching off from the former, and uniting with the latter, 60 m. ESE of its confluence with the canal of Casiquiari.

BARIAT, or BIRAT, a conical mountain of Hindostan, in the prov. of Ghurwal, situated between the rivers Jumna and Tonse, in N lat. 30° 35', and E long. 77° 55'. It rises to the height of 6,508 ft. above the level of Saharunpoor; and is surmounted by a fortification. The summit is covered throughout winter with snow.
BARIGAZZO, a town of Italy, in the duchy of Modena, remarkable for a burning vapour which issues from the surface of the ground on a spot in the vicinity.
BARIGIANO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo Ultra, in the dist. and 12 m. ESE of Aquila, situated on a hill. It is the cap. of a cant., and has one annual fair. Pop. 1,871.

BARILE, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Bacupies an agreeable situation on the acclivity of a hill. It was founded by a Greek colony, and, until the 17th cent., the prevailing religion was that of the Greek church. It has one annual fair. Pop. 3,730.

BARIMA (Point), a headland of English Guayana, forming the southern extremity of the Navios estuary of the Orinoco, in N lat. 8° 44' 30", 12 m. W of Point Sabaneta, the Punta Barima of the Spanish charts.

BARIMO CANO, a river of English Guayana, which takes its rise in the Sierra Imataca, runs in a NNE direction, and, after inosculating with the Barama, or Upper Guayana, by the Wabarina, below the parallel of 8°, bends to the NW, again inosculates with the Guayana by what is called the Mora passage, and insulating a low and narrow tract of land about 60 m. in length, flows into the Navios mouth of the Orinoco, in N lat. 8° 42'.
BARIN, a Mongolian encampment on the headstreams of the Khara-Mouren, and western flank of the Kingan mountains, 280 m. NNE of Peking.
BARINAGOTOS, a tribe and territory of Guayana, in the intendency of Orinoco, extending from the southern bank of the Avechica to the Acanum.
BARINAS. See VARINAS.
BARING'S ISLES, a cluster of low islets of the Caroline archipelago in the N Pacific, in the Radiack group, in N lat. 5° 40', and E long. 168° 15'.
BARIQUISIMETO. See BARQUISIMETO.
BARISCIANO. See Barigiano.

BARISH, a river of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Simbirsk, which takes its rise in the S of the dist. of Syzran, passes Karsoun, and, after a course in a general direction from SE to NW of 120 m., falls into the Soura. This river formerly constituted the boundary between the territory of the Nogais Tatars and Russia.
BARISIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aisne, and cant. of Coucy-le-Chateau. Pop. 1,207.
BARISZ, a town of Galicia, in the circle and 26 m. NE of Stanislawow. It has a castle, a Catholic, and a Greek church. Pop. 1,820.

BARJAC, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Gard, arrond. of Alais. The cant. comprises 7 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 5,250. The town is situated in a mountainous district, on the NE confines of the dep., 20 m. NE of Alais, and 35 m. N of Nismes. It has a mineral spring. Pop. 1,975.—Also a commune in the dep. of Lozere, and cant. of Chanac. Pop. 1,007.

BARJOLS, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Var, arrond. of Brignolles. The cant. comprises 11 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 9,099. The town is 12 m. NNW of Brignolles, and 35 NE of Marseilles. It possesses a subterranean chapel—formerly belonging to a Carmelite convent—remarkable for the beauty of the stalactite incrustations with which it is adorned, and several caverns, similarly adorned, which afford excellent sand for glass manufacture. It contains distilleries of brandy, oil-presses, manufactories of vermicelli, silk-spinning and paper mills, china manufactories, and tanneries; and has 3 annual fairs. The environs produce figs, raisins, and olives. Pop. 3,512.
BARKA, a village of Hungary, in the comitat and 6 m. WNW of Torna. It has mines of copper and iron, and iron forges.

BARKA, or Burka, a town of Arabia, in the prov. of Oman, in the imamaut and 45 m. WNW of Muskat, and 60 m. ENE of Rostak. It extends about a mile along the coast; and is defended by a fort of considerable extent in the immediate vicinity, in which the imaum of Muscat occasionally resides. The environs, which are level and sandy, produce dates in great abundance.—Also a group of islands BAR in the gulf of Oman, in N lat. 23° 40', and E long. 57° 50'.
BARKAH. See BARCA.
BARKAH, a town of Central Egypt, in the dist. and 33 m. SW of Beni-Yousuf, 18 m. N of Balmeseh, on the Bahr-Yousuf.

BARKAID, a town of Turkey in Asia, in the gov. and 25 m. S of Algezira, and 125 SE of Diarkebir. BARKA'L, BERKAL, OF BARGAL (JEBEL), a remarkable hill in Nubia, in N lat. 18° 31', about 62 m. NE of Dongola. It is a tabular mass of sandstone, nearly 400 ft. high, and about 25 minutes' walk in circuit, rising abruptly from the level of the Desert, at the distance of about half-a-mile from the W bank of the Nile, and presenting on its S side, towards the river, a perpendicular face of nearly 200 ft. high, broken by a number of rugged fissures. On its summit are the remains of several temples; and in the vicinity, towards the W, are the remains of 17 pyramids, the largest of which has a base of about 81 ft. square; they are composed of earth and rubbish. To the SE are some remains of Grecian architecture. - Cailliaud. -Ruppell. - Waddington.
BARKBY, a parish in Leicestershire, 5 m. NE of Leicester. Area, with the chapelries of Barkbythorpe and North Thurmaston, 2,290 acres. Pop. 849.
BARKHAUSEN, a village of Westphalia, in the gov. and 3 m. S of Minden, near the l. bank of the Weser. The environs abound with coal, which has been worked since 1663.

BAR-KHOTON, or Bars-Koto, a town of Mongolia, in the territory of the Kalkhas, on the l. bank of the Keroulan, 140 m. WSW of its entrance into Lake Kulon, and 600 m. NNW of Peking. It is now greatly dilapidated.
BARK-HUT-PLAINS, a dist. of Van Diemen's Land, in the prov. and to the W of the river Clyde, and bounded on the W by the Blue-Hill range.
BARKER (Mount), a mountain in South Australia, between Lake Victoria and the town of Adelaide.

BARKING, a parish and town in Essex, 7 m. ENE of London. Area of the parish, which comprises the wards, as they are called, of Barking town, Chadwell, Great Ilford, and Ripple, 10,170 acres. Pop. in 1841, 8,718, exclusive of 980 sea-farers. -The town stands on the small river Roding, about 2 m. above its junction with the Thames. A large proportion of its inhabitants are fishermen, or are engaged as bargemen in conveying coal and timber on the Thames. Pop. of the town in 1801, 1,585: in 1841, 3,751. The Roding, which is of considerable breadth below the town, and known in that part as Barking creek, is navigable by small boats to Ilford. A large portion of the p., called the Level, is beneath the level of high tide mark; and is only protected from inundation by an immense embankment from 8 to 14 ft. in height.—Also a p. in Suffolk, 1 m. SW of Needham. Area 3,010 acres. Pop., with the chapelry of Needham Market, and the hamlet of Darmsden, 1,879.
BARKISLAND, a township in the parish of Halifax, W. R. of Yorkshire, 41 m. SE of Halifax. Area 2,420 acres. Pop. 2,391.

BARKOL, or PAR KOL, a lake of the Chinese empire, in the prov. of the Khalkha Mongols, in N lat. 43° 50', E long. 94° 20', 60 m. N of Khamil and the snowy mountains of Artokhoi, in the Tinshan chain.—There is a considerable town of the same name a little to the S of it, called by the Chinese SHIN-SI-FU.

BARKSTONE, a parish of Leicestershire, 11 m. N by W of Melton Mowbray. Area 2,870 acres. Pop. 403.—Also a p. in Lincolnshire, 4 m. NNE of Grantham. Area 2,080 acres. Pop. 413.—Also a township in the p. of Sherburn, in the W. R. of Yorkshire. Area 1,260 acres. Pop. 323.

BARKSTONE-ASH, a wapentake in the W. R. of Yorkshire, lying between the rivers Aire and Ouse. Its upper division has an area of 39,040 acres; the lower division, 45,600 acres. Pop. of the former in 1841, 12,007; of the latter, 14,999.
BARKWAY, a parish and town in Hertfordshire, 13 m. NNE of Hertford. Area of p. 5,060 acres. Pop., with the hamlets of Newsells and Nuthampstead, 1,291. Pop. of the town in 1801, 699; in 1841, 847.
BARKWITH (EAST and WEST), two adjoining parishes in Lincolnshire, 3 m. NE of Wragby. Area of E. B. 990 acres. Pop. 255. Area of W. B. 500 acres. Pop. 130.
BARLAH, or BARLEH, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in Anatolia, in the sanjak of Hamid, near the E side of Lake Egerdir. It is celebrated for its raisins.
BARLASSONA, a village of Lombardy, in the deleg. and 12 m. N. of Milan, on the Seveso. There is an ecclesiastical seminary here. Pop. 1,000.
BARLASTON, a parish in Staffordshire, 3 m. N by W of Stone. Area 2,340 acres. Pop. 591.
BARLAVINGTON, a parish in Sussex, 4 m. S of Petworth. Area, with Burton, 1,710 acres. Pop. 139.
